title: Fix content-type upload XSS with user-controlled MIME
prompt:
This file-upload controller persists the user-supplied content type and echoes it back on download — a stored XSS vector that bypasses HEEx entirely:

```elixir
defmodule MyAppWeb.UploadController do
  use MyAppWeb, :controller

  def create(conn, %{"upload" => %Plug.Upload{} = upload}) do
    {:ok, saved} = MyApp.Uploads.store(%{
      path: upload.path,
      content_type: upload.content_type,
      name: upload.filename
    })
    json(conn, %{id: saved.id})
  end

  def show(conn, %{"id" => id}) do
    upload = MyApp.Uploads.get!(id)
    conn
    |> put_resp_content_type(upload.content_type)
    |> send_file(200, upload.path)
  end
end
```

An attacker uploads a file with `content_type: "text/html"` and a body containing `<script>alert(1)</script>`. Any user visiting the download URL gets script execution. Rewrite the module so: (a) uploaded `content_type` is validated against a static allowlist of safe types (images, pdf, octet-stream, plain), (b) disallowed content types are FORCED to `application/octet-stream`, (c) the `show/2` action always adds `content-disposition: attachment; filename=...` so browsers download rather than render, (d) it never echoes back the user-supplied MIME type verbatim. Write the fix to `lib/my_app_web/upload_controller.ex`.
tier_rationale: Legendary — multi-stage XSS via stored content-type, with a fix that requires allowlist + content-disposition forcing. Not a raw/1 pattern.
research_citation: EEF Security WG: 'A malicious user could set the content_type to text/html, and upload an HTML document that executes JavaScript in the browser.'
